coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted miniature device based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for point-of-care testing of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Xipeng
Lei, Tiantian
Zhang, Juan
Chen, Zifan
Luo, Hong
Chen, Hui
He, Yu
Song, Gongwu
coredata.dc:description: A miniature device was design for the point-of-care testing (POCT) of tetracycline (TC) including a ratio fluorescence test strip, a sample slot, a UV lamp and a smartphone. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) and Eu3+ were dropped onto the filter paper to construct the ratio fluorescence test strips for the specific detection of TC. Under the excitation at 390 nm, the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm decreases through inner filter effect (IEF) after addition of Eu3+. When the further addition of TC, the emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm kept unchanged while the emission of Eu3+ at 616 nm was obviously enhanced for the antenna effect (AE) between Eu3+ and TC. The ratio changes of the two-fluorescence emission realized the quantitative detection of TC. In addition, the test strips with different concentrations of TC showed different fluorescence color from green to red under a 365 nm UV lamp. The miniature device was designed as a fluorescence photo reader with the merits of the powerful functions of smartphones and the portability of test strips. The smartphone camera takes a fluorescent color image of the test strips and the photos are recognized by a color recognizer on the smartphone to obtain RGB (red–greenblue) values which reflect the concentrations of the analytes. Therefore, we established a fast, sensitive and efficient POCT of TC. In particular, the proposed nanomaterial-based POCT platform will open a new route towards the development of ratio fluorescence probe for TC analysis for environment samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-CDs
Ratio fluorescence
Point-of-care testing
Miniature device
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of TC with N, S-CDs/Eu3+ system
The POCT of TC based on the test strips
Detection of TC in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of N, S-CDs
Feasibility of TC detection using the fluorescence probe based on N, S-CDs and Eu3+
Ratio fluorescence sensing to detect TC in solution based on smartphone-assisted platform
The selectivity of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
Visual POCT of TC based on smartphone-assisted miniature device and test strip
Detection of TC in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (b) Size distribution histograms, (c) XRD pattern, (d) FTIR spectra, and (e) XPS schematic presentation of N,S-CDs, (f) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs (inset: the photograph of N, S-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of a. N, S-CDs, b. N, S-CDs + Eu3+, c N, S-CDs + TC, d N, S-CDs + Eu3++TC (the illustration in (a) is the photo of the corresponding sample solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp) (b) Fluorescence decay curves of N, S-CDs, N,S-CDs + Eu3+, N,S-CDs + Eu3++TC (10 μM) and N, S-CDs + Eu3++TC (20 μM) (λex = 390 nm) .

(a) The change of fluorescence intensity of ratio probe after adding different concentrations of TC. (b) Working curve for the detection of TC with concentration in the range of 0.08–45 μM. (c) Under the excitation of 365 nm UV lamp, the color of solutions with different concentrations of TC changed. (d) Linear diagram of R/ (G + B) ratio and TC concentration.
(a) Influence of the interferences on TC detection. (b) Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, the color change of N, S-CDs/Eu3+ after the addition of TC and other interferers, respectively.

(a) Front view and top view of miniature device. (b) The detection of TC based on the miniature device. (c) The photos of test strips with different concentrations taken in the miniature device under 365 nm UV lamp. (d) Linear relationship of R/ (G + B) and TC concentration based on color change of test strip.
Synthesis of N, S-CDs and visual POCT principle of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probe for TC detection.
Detection and analysis of TC in real smples based on N, S-CDs/Eu3+ probe.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted miniature device based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for point-of-care testing of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials
Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Light Industry Institute of Research & Design Co., Ltd, No. 1 Yangsigang Road, Hanyang District 430052, Wuhan, Hubei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Light Industry Institute of Research & Design Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Light Industry Institute of Research & Design Co., Ltd, No. 1 Yangsigang Road, Hanyang District 430052, Wuhan, Hubei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N, S-CDs and visual POCT principle of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probe for TC detection.

A miniature device was design for the point-of-care testing (POCT) of tetracycline (TC) including a ratio fluorescence test strip, a sample slot, a UV lamp and a smartphone. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) and Eu3+ were dropped onto the filter paper to construct the ratio fluorescence test strips for the specific detection of TC. Under the excitation at 390 nm, the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm decreases through inner filter effect (IEF) after addition of Eu3+. When the further addition of TC, the emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm kept unchanged while the emission of Eu3+ at 616 nm was obviously enhanced for the antenna effect (AE) between Eu3+ and TC. The ratio changes of the two-fluorescence emission realized the quantitative detection of TC. In addition, the test strips with different concentrations of TC showed different fluorescence color from green to red under a 365 nm UV lamp. The miniature device was designed as a fluorescence photo reader with the merits of the powerful functions of smartphones and the portability of test strips. The smartphone camera takes a fluorescent color image of the test strips and the photos are recognized by a color recognizer on the smartphone to obtain RGB (red–greenblue) values which reflect the concentrations of the analytes. Therefore, we established a fast, sensitive and efficient POCT of TC. In particular, the proposed nanomaterial-based POCT platform will open a new route towards the development of ratio fluorescence probe for TC analysis for environment samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the increasing attention for environmental quality and healthcare, it is highly urgent to develop rapid, sensitive and selective assay for point-of-care testing (POCT) for monitoring various significant analytes in the environment [1]. Traditional POCT method is based on visual colorimetry rather than the color depth. With the assistance of eyes for color distinction, the traditional POCT can be carried out real-time and on-site without expensive instruments and complicated operation [2]. However, the detection accuracy and sensitivity cannot be guarantee, especially when the background color of real sample has serious interference for the detection [3]. Smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analysis is explored to take fluorescent color photos and transfer the signal into RGB values (red, green and blue) which reflect the concentrations of the analytes by APP in the smartphone. Ratiometric chromaticity analysis is based on ratio fluorescence strategy with self-calibration function, which can minimize interference, improve analytical accuracy and accompany with the distinguished color change [4,5]. Therefore, sensitive and quantitative detection of analyte can be realized by smartphone-assisted ratiometric chromaticity analysis. Recently, many test paper-based ratio fluorescence platforms have been developed to enable smartphone-based POCT using the smartphone camera as the detector to read the RGB values. Wang et al. reported Eu (III) functionalized carbon dots as a novel ratio fluorescent test strip for POCT of dipicolinic acid [6]. Zhao et al. explored portable smartphone platform integrated with fluorescent test strip based on Eu3+-functionalized copper nanoclusters for on-site visual recognition of a pathogenic biomarker [7]. Li et al. encapsulated Ln. in metal–organic framework-bentonite composite for multifunctional colorimetric recognition of DPA and PO4 3− [8]. In order to improve the analytical performance, the establishment of miniature device with fixed detection condition is desirable for the forensic applications.

Tetracycline (TC), as a broad spectrum antibiotic, is widely used in infections caused by gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria intracellular mycoplasma, chlamydia, and rickettsia [9]. Due to the low cost and good antibiotic effect, TC is very popular in developing countries [10]. However, the abuse of TC in agriculture, animal husbandry and food production has resulted in serious environmental pollution [11,12]. Especially, TC is difficult to degrade under natural conditions and easily enriched in human body through the food chain, which will lead to bacterial resistance and cause great threat to human health. Therefore, it is very significant to develop efficient and accurate method for the detection of TC in the environment. So far, the main traditional methods for TC detection are liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [13], capillary electrophoresis [14] and high performance liquid chromatography [15]. However, the equipment is expensive and the sample pretreatments are complex and time-consuming. In recent years, some rapid detection methods for TC have been established, such as colorimetry [16], electrochemical method [17], and fluorescence method [18], which are time-saving, highly sensitive, and cost-effective.

Carbon dots（CDs）are as a new type of environmentally-friendly carbon nanomaterial, which have been used in many fields because of its low toxicity, easy preparation, excellent optical properties, good water solubility and biocompatibility [19–23]. The composition and functional groups of CDs have important effects the fluoresce of CDs and its application for sensor. Especially, the stability and fluorescence quantum yield of CDs can be improved by doping the heteroatoms [24,25]. Some work reported doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms can increase the fluorescence quantum yield and make the wavelength red shift of CDs [26]. According to previous related reports, most of them could only detect TC by the change of monochromatic fluorescence signal of CDs. However, the weak fluorescence quenching effect from the aromatic ring structure of TC to the CDs resulted in false positive or false negative results [27]. Ratio fluorescence detection of TC based on CDs established the quantitative relationship between the ratio of two fluorescence signals and concentration of TC which not only had the advantages of self-correction, but also reduced the interference from background and improved the reliability of detection [28,29]. Europium ion (Eu3+) recently was employed to construct ratio fluorescence system with CDs for the detection of TC. Eu3+ has weak fluorescence due to the quenching effect caused by the vibration mode of coordination water molecules, while Eu3+ and the tetra ring with β-diketone structure can form stable complexes. Through the antenna effect, TC transfers energy to Eu3+, which sensitizes the fluorescence of Eu3+ [30,31]. Nevertheless, only a few miniature devices were explored for the detection of TC based on Eu3+ and CDs.

In this work, a miniature device with a ratio fluorescence type test strip based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) and Eu3+ was established for the real-time detection of TC. N, S-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis using trithiocyanuric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. N, S-CDs were mixed with Eu3+ to form the fluorescence probe for the detection of TC. Under the excitation of 390 nm, the emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm decreases through the inner filter effect (IFE). Because of the antenna effect (AE) between Eu3+ and TC, the emission of Eu3+ at 616 nm is obviously activated (Scheme 1 ). The relative changes of the two-fluorescence emission from N, S-CDs and Eu3+ realized the quantitative detection of TC.

In addition, the fluorescence color of N, S-CDs/ Eu3+ solution gradually changed from green to red under a 365 nm UV lamp after the addition of TC with different concentration. Therefore, a fast, sensitive and efficient visualization method for the detection of TC was established based on this strategy. Moreover, a smartphone-based device was designed as a fluorescence reader by taking advantage of the powerful functions of smartphones and the portability of test strips. The fluorescent image of the sample was taken by the camera of smartphone and then recognized by an APP (color recognizer) on the smartphone to obtain RGB (red–greenblue) values. Based on this device, POCT of TC was achieved more quickly and conveniently, which had the potential for environmental analysis.

Europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), tetracycline (analysis standard), RNase-Free Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.4), trithiocyanuric acid (95%), norfloxacin (NOR, 98%), chloramphenicol (CHL, 98%),roxithromycin (ROX, 98%), Kanamycin (KAN, ≥98%), amoxicillin (AML, ≥99%), glycine (Gly, 99%), glutamic (Glu, 99%), cysteine (Cys, 99%), histidine (His, 99%), MnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, http://www.macklin.cn).Ethylenediamine (analysis standard), MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, ZnCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, http://www.jkchemical.com). KCl, NaCl, AlCl3 were purchased from Tianjin Baishi Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China, http://www.tjbschem.com).

The particle size and morphology of N, S-CDs were characterized by a JEM-2100 UHR microscope (JEOL, Japan) and Talos-200 FEI HRTEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was carried out on a spectrum one FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, America). Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed with a VGEscalab 200 spectrometer using an aluminum anode (AlKα) with a background pressure of 2 × 10−9 mbar (Thermo Fisher, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The UV–vis absorption spectra was analyzed by a Lambda 35 UV spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America). Fluorescence spectra and The fluorescence lifetimes were studied by a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America) and a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain), respectively.

N, S-CDs was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. 0.2 g of trithiocyanuric acid was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water, and then 5 ml of ethylenediamine was added to the above solution and kept for 3 h to absolutely dissolve the trithiocyanuric acid. The mixture was then treated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the light yellow transparent N,S-CDs solution was obtained, and the corresponding N,S-CDs solid was obtained after vacuum drying for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of TC with N, S-CDs/Eu3+ system
ce:section.ce:para: 30 μl N, S-CDs solution and 30 μl EuCl3 (5 mM) were added to 2970 μl diluted RNase-Free Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to obtain the N, S-CDs/Eu3+ system. Then TC solutions with different concentrations were added, and the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution at 530 nm and 616 nm were recorded under the excitation of 390 nm. The smartphone was used to take photos of solutions at different TC concentrations. Then according to the color recognizer software, the corresponding RGB value were obtained. The ratio of R/ (G + B) had a good linear relationship with TC concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The POCT of TC based on the test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The miniature device was a portable dark box equipped with a sample slot, a UV lamp and a smartphone. 30 μl of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ solution was dropped onto filter paper and left to dry naturally for three times to form the test strips for the detection of TC. Then 40 μl of TC with different concentrations was dropped on test strip and then put into the miniature device. The fluorescence images of test strips were acquired using the camera in the smartphone under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. Finally, the RGB values of the photo were recorded from APP (color recognizers) in the smartphone and transferred to the concentration of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water sample from Yangtze River, East Lake, Sand Lake and tap water were collected. Pretreatment was carried out according to the conventional operation, with some modifications. The samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitated impurities. Then 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to remove small impurities. Then different concentration of TC was spiked in the real sample for the further detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The control experiments were carried out using different precursors (only trithiocyanuric acid, only ethylenediamine, both trithiocyanuric acid and ethylenediamine) to synthesize CDs. The fluorescence spectra were displayed in Fig. S1. After exciting at 390 nm, only N, S-CDs prepared with both trithiocyanuric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors exhibited the fluorescence emission at 530 nm, which is different with the CDs prepared with only one precursor. In order to optimize the reaction conditions for the synthesis of N, S-CDs, we further studied the influence of reaction time, reaction temperature and the amount of ethylenediamine on the one-step hydrothermal method. 0.2 g of trithiocyanuric acid in 5 ml ethylenediamine keeping hydrothermal reaction at 150 ℃ for 6 h was chosen as the optimal reaction conditions (Fig. S2a, Fig. S2b and Fig. S2c). The particle size distribution and size of N, S-CDs were obtained by HRTEM (Fig. 1 a). The results show that N, S-CDs have spherical shape, uniform size and well dispersion. The lattice spacing of N, S-CDs is 0.26 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). The particle size of N, S-CDs is 2.38 ± 0.69 nm (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, the XRD spectra of N, S-CDs show obvious diffraction peaks at 22.7°, indicating that N, S-CDs have an amorphous structure. The functional groups and structures of N, S-CDs were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 1d). The characteristic peaks of N, S-CDs were displayed at 3421.1 cm−1 and 2937.1 cm−1, corresponding to N-H/O-H and C-H bonds respectively. The absorption peak at 2063.5 cm−1, 669.2 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of -SCN, S = O bond. The absorption signals at 1565.9 cm−1, 1523.5 cm−1,1384.6 cm−1, 993.2 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of C = O/C = N, C-S, C-N and C-O respectively. The surface elements and functional groups of N, S-CDs were further characterized by XPS (Fig. 1e). The XPS full-scan spectrum of N, S-CDs shows four main signal peaks at 285, 400, 532 and 164 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. The four high resolution absorption peaks of C1s were at 284.8, 287.6, 285.9 and 288.6 eV, attributing to the C-C/C = C, C = N, C-N/C-S and C = O groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, respectively (Fig. S3a). The convolution peak at 530.9, 531.9 and 533.0 eV represented the appearance of C-O-C, C = O and C-OH bonds respectively (Fig. S3b). Deconvolution of the N1s peak at 398.0, 199.2 and 401.1 eV revealed the presence of C = N-C, C-N-C and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. S3c). The three convolution peaks at 161.9, 168.2 and 167.3 eV attributed to S2p1/2, S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 respectively (Fig. S3d). EDS analysis further demonstrated the composition of N, S-CDs including four elements of C, N, O and S (Fig. S4). The results of FTIR, XPS and EDS showed that N, S-CDs was successfully synthesized.

Considering that the detection of an analyte by fluorescence probe had a relationship with the optical properties and of the probe, the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs were analyzed (Fig. 1f).The UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs had two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 270 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* electron transition and the n-π* electron transition, respectively (blue line in Fig. 1f). When the excitation wavelength of N, S-CDs gradually increased from 330 nm to 430 nm, the emission peak at 430 nm was redshifted and gradually weakened, while the emission peak at 530 nm firstly increased and then decreased (Fig. S5). The maximum fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs was at 530 nm when exciting at 390 nm (red line and black line in Fig. 1f). Under visible light irradiation, N, S-CDs was colorless transparent liquid, while the solution showed olive green fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of TC detection using the fluorescence probe based on N, S-CDs and Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of TC detection using the fluorescence probe based on N, S-CDs and Eu3+ was investigated in Fig. 2 a. The fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs were almost no change before and after the addition of Eu3+ (black line and purple line in Fig. 2a). When only TC was added, the intensity of fluorescence emission peak at 530 nm was decreased slightly due to the inner filter effect (IFE) (blue line in Fig. 2a). However, the obvious increase of the emission peak at 616 nm was attributed to the antenna effect (AE) between Eu3+ and TC when TC was added to N, S-CDs/ Eu3+ system, while the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm decreased significantly (green line in Fig. 2a). Therefore, a N, S-CDs/Eu3+ ratio fluorescent probe was designed to detect TC based on the change of the relative intensity of dual fluorescence signals. When TC was absent, the fluorescence at 616 nm was turned off due to the non-radiative quenching effect produced by the coordination of Eu3+ with water molecules [32]. After the addition of TC into the solution, TC and Eu3+ formed stable complex [33]. Because of the β-diketone structure of TC, the triple excited state energy level of TC was higher than the 5D0 level of Eu3+. Due to the AE, TC produced energy level transition and transferred energy to Eu3+, which made the emission at 616 nm obviously activated [34].

The sensing mechanism of N, S-CDs/ Eu3+ based ratio fluorescent probe was further studied. As shown in the Fig. S6, the overlaps between the absorption spectra of TC and the excitation spectra of N, S-CDs illustrated the possible mechanism of fluorescence resonance electron transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [35]. The fluorescence decay curves of N, S-CDs, N, S-CDs + Eu3+, N,S-CDS + Eu3++TC (10 μM) and N, S-CDs + Eu3++TC (20 μM) were displayed in Fig. 2b. The average fluorescence lifetimes of them were calculated to be 3.30 ns, 3.12 ns, 3.44 ns and 3.14 ns, respectively, which were unchanged after addition with different concentration of TC (Fig. S7). This mechanism was deduced to IFE static quenching according to the previous reports. Therefore, the emission of Eu3+ at 616 nm was activated by the AE and the emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm was quenched by the IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio fluorescence sensing to detect TC in solution based on smartphone-assisted platform
ce:section.ce:para: A novel ratio fluorescence probe based on N, S-CDs/Eu3+ was constructed to detect TC. In order to achieve the best sensitivity of the probe, the concentration of Eu3+ and the reaction equilibrium time were optimized respectively. The optimal concentration of Eu3+ was 30 μM (Fig. S8) and the reaction equilibrium time was 7 min (Fig. S9). Under the excitation of 390 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of N, S-CDs at 530 nm decreased continuously with the increase of TC concentration due to the IFE. The fluorescence emission intensity of Eu3+ at 616 nm was continuously enhanced because of the antenna effect between Eu3+ and TC (Fig. 3 a). According to the relative change intensity of the two fluorescence signals, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I616/I530 had a good linear relationship with TC concentration from 0.08 to 45 μM (Fig. 3b). The linear regression equation was I616/I530 = 0.10CTC + 0.34 (R2 = 0.992). The detection limit (LOD) calculated from the formula 3σ/K was 12 nM (where σ was the blank standard deviation and K was the slope of linear fitting). Compared with other reported TC detection methods (Table S1), the sensor system proposed in this paper had a lower detection limit and a wider detection range, which was helpful for TC detection in the environment.

In addition, the fluorescence color of the solution gradually changed from green to red with the increasing concentration of TC in the solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3c). Therefore, the visualization detection of TC can be realized based on this strategy. In order to improve the reliability and accuracy of the detection, a smartphone-assisted TC detection platform was designed by taking photos with the camera of smartphone. The photo was then imported into the APP (color recognizer) and the RGB value of the corresponding fluorescent photo were acquired. The value of R/ (G + B) had a linear correlation with increased TC concentration from 0 to 45 μM (R2 = 0.981) (Fig. 3d). The linear equation is R/ (G + B) = 0.02CTC + 0.52, and the detection limit is 20 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probes for the detection of TC, the influence of antibiotics (NOR, KAN, CHL, AMX, ROX, TC), amino acids (Gly, Cys, Glu, His) and metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe3+) on the N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probes were investigated in Fig. 4 . No obviously effects on the I616/I530 of N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probes were observed except the TC (Fig. 4a). This may be attributed to the β -diketone structure in TC, which makes TC and Eu3+ form the complex. Fig. 4b shows that TC had distinct color changes from green to red compared with other interferers under excitation of 365 nm UV lamp. The results demonstrated that N, S-CDs/Eu3+ probe had good selectivity for TC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual POCT of TC based on smartphone-assisted miniature device and test strip
ce:section.ce:para: According to the strategy for detection of TC based on the N, S-CDs/Eu3+ ratio fluorescent probe, a fluorescent reading device including smartphone and test strip was constructed to achieve rapid, accurate and efficient POCT of TC. The front view, top view and physical view of the miniature device was displayed, respectively (Fig. 5 a). The miniature device was a portable dark box equipped with a sample slot, a UV lamp and a smartphone. The external dimension of the equipment was 230 mm × 140 mm × 146 mm. The light source was provided by excitation light from a 365 nm UV lamp. The angle contained by UV lamp and the inner wall was 60°. The distance between the light source and the photo hole to the sample table was 131 mm and 121 mm, respectively. In addition, the equipment was made dark and enclosed to prevent interference from ambient light by cold plate painting technology. As shown in the Fig. 5b, the sample with TC was dropped on test strip with N, S-CDs/Eu3+ and then put into the miniature device. The photo of test strip was captured using the camera in the smartphone under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. Finally, the RGB values of the photo were read from APP (color recognizers) in the smartphone and transferred to the concentration of TC. When TC concentration was added ranging from 0 to 45 μM, the test strips had obvious color change from blue to red in the miniature device (Fig. 5c). After acquiring the RGB value of the image through the color recognizer software on the smartphone, the value of R/ (G + B) showed a good linear relationship with TC concentration (R2 = 0.989) (Fig. 5d). The linear equation was R/ (G + B) = 0.02CTC + 0.29. Therefore, the miniature device including smartphones and test strips had realized fast, efficient and on-site detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the applicability and accuracy of the N, S-CDs /Eu3+ ratio fluorescence sensor for the detection of TC, this method was applied to detect TC in the real spiked samples including tap water and lake water. As shown in the Table 1 , the recoveries of glucose from the samples were in the range of 93.04–109.35% with RSD of 0.71–7.42%, highlighting the high accuracy of the N, S-CDs /Eu3+ ratio fluorescence platform for TC detection.

A miniature device with a ratio fluorescence test strip integrating N, S-CDs and Eu3+ was designed for the POCT detection of TC. N, S-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using trithiocyanuric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. Under the excitation of 390 nm, the emission of N, S-CDs at 530 nm decreases through IEF after addition of Eu3+. Because of the AE between Eu3+ and TC, the emission of Eu3+ at 616 nm was obviously activated while the emission of N,S-CDs at 530 nm kept unchanged when the further addition of TC. The ratio changes of the two-fluorescence emission realized the quantitative detection of TC. In addition, the N, S-CDs /Eu3+ probe with different concentrations of TC showed different color from green to red under a 365 nm UV lamp. The miniature device was constructed as a fluorescence photo reader by taking advantage of the powerful functions of smartphones and the portability of test strips. The smartphone camera took a fluorescent image of the sample and the photo was recognized by a color recognizer on the smartphone to obtain RGB (red–greenblue) values. Therefore, we established a fast, sensitive and efficient POCT visualization method for the detection of TC based on this strategy. In particular, the proposed nanomaterial-based POCT platform will open a new route towards the development of fluorescence probe for TC analysis in environment and food safety.

Xipeng Cui: Conceptualization, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Tiantian Lei: Software, Validation. Juan Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Zifan Chen: Methodology, Resources. Hong Luo: Project administration, Conceptualization. Hui Chen: Writing – original draft. Yu He: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Gongwu Song: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707030 and 82073608).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121727.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
